heading: SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO LXXIX
card_summary: This chunk from Campanini's introductory essay to De Harmonia Mundi addresses Zorzi's doctrine of Hebrew as the Adamic language — the divinely instituted lingua prima whose word-thing correspondences survived Babel intact. Drawing on Josephus, Augustine, and the kabbalistic concept of a celestial alphabet inscribed on the throne of God, Zorzi argues that Hebrew was both the first language and will be the last, spoken by the blessed in the heavenly city. Campanini situates Zorzi in dialogue with the Jewish Aristotelian grammarian Avraham De Balmes (Miqneh Avram) and traces connections to Plato's Cratylus, the Humanist recovery of Greek, and the later tradition of Egyptian hieroglyphic mysticism running from Orapollo through Bruno to Kircher. The passage culminates in the claim that Hebrew is not merely the oldest surviving language but the eschatological horizon of all knowledge — the score on which the harmony of the world was first, is now, and will finally be played.
essay:
## Hebrew as the Adamic and Eschatological Language

This section of Campanini's *Saggio introduttivo* (pp. 79–83) is devoted to one of the most structurally important arguments in *De Harmonia Mundi*: Zorzi's claim that Hebrew is the *prima lingua* — the original, divinely ordained language of Adam — and that its perfection, far from being a matter of mere historical priority, constitutes both the foundation and the eschatological telos of all human knowledge and worship.

## The Adamic Language and the Superiority of Hebrew

Zorzi grounds the privileged status of Hebrew in its unbroken correspondence between words and things (*consequentia rerum*), a correspondence that was preserved intact through Babel while all other languages were corrupted. Campanini explains that in a Renaissance intellectual culture structured around genealogy, establishing priority was essential for establishing authority. Zorzi accordingly marshals apologetic sources — citing Josephus — to attribute the invention of the alphabet itself to the Jews, who then transmitted it to the Babylonians, Egyptians, and Phoenicians, along with astrology and authentic wisdom (p. 79, n. 340).

The argument is framed explicitly against the background of Plato's *Cratylus*. Zorzi explains why Socratic etymologies appear uncertain and the mapping of signifier to signified is often opaque in Greek: Plato was attempting to imitate the etymology of the *prima lingua*, but Greek lacks Hebrew's divinely ordained congruence between names and things. As the text states:

> Platone, infatti, volle imitare l'etimologia della lingua originaria [prima lingua] nella quale la prima creatura [protoplastes] impose alle cose nomi perfettamente adeguati. Tuttavia la corrispondenza tra i nomi e le cose in ebraico è tanto diversa da quella che si verifica in greco e in latino, quanto la stessa lingua ebraica è più santa e divina di queste altre lingue, che la seguono a notevole distanza: la lingua latina assai più della greca, perché quest'ultima deriva direttamente dall'ebraico, mentre il latino deriva a sua volta dal greco.

<cite>p. 79–80</cite>

This establishes a strict hierarchy: Hebrew → Greek → Latin, with holiness and semantic precision diminishing at each remove.

## The Sanctity of Hebrew's Graphic Form

Zorzi's veneration of Hebrew extends to every dimension of the written text: not only the semantic structure but the letters, vowel points (*nikud*), accents, and diacritical marks are all held to be sacred and laden with meaning. Campanini notes that in this Zorzi is fully aligned with Hebrew esoteric doctrine (*secretiores theologi Hebraeorum*) and explicitly departs from the traditional Christian suspicion of Hebrew documented already in Augustine (p. 80, n. 343). The *secretiores theologi* — the kabbalists — are Zorzi's authorities for the claim that the permutations of letters, apices, and gematria-calculations can explain all things. The celestial alphabet inscribed on the throne of God served as the model for the Torah letters that Moses first wrote down.

## Hebrew as the Language of Paradise: Past and Future

The most philosophically ambitious move in this passage is Zorzi's argument that Hebrew is not only the language of the terrestrial paradise but will be the language of the heavenly city (*patria ultraterrena*). After the resurrection, the blessed will praise the Archetype with their restored vocal organs — and they will do so in Hebrew. Campanini quotes an extended passage from *De Harmonia Mundi* to illustrate this:

> Perciò occorre esaminare quale lingua o idioma [i beati] parlano. A me pare ragionevole al di là di ogni dubbio che, se fanno ricorso a un idioma, esso sarà certamente l'ebraico. In ebraico è stata rivelata la legge, è stato predicato il vangelo da Cristo, sono stati scritti gli inni divini e sono state pronunciate le profezie, fatta eccezione per quella rivelazione che si chiama con nome greco Apocalisse, la quale fu composta, e ricevuta, in greco. L'ebraico è una lingua colma di misteri: la forma delle lettere, i punti, gli accenti e le cifre corrispondenti a ciascuna lettera tanto che i teologi esoterici [secretiores theologi] affermano di poter spiegare tutte le cose mediante le permutazioni delle lettere e degli apici e per mezzo dei calcoli applicati a quelle cifre.

<cite>pp. 80–81</cite>

Hebrew's eschatological status is reinforced by the argument that the language was preserved through Babel in a pious family — identified via Augustine with Eber (whence *Hebraeus*), from whose line Abraham descended. The etymology of the name Abraham is glossed through the root *'abar* (עבר), meaning "to pass over." The celestial intelligences themselves, when addressing someone who knows the language, speak Hebrew; authentic oracles have all been delivered in it. The passage culminates in a political-theological metaphor: just as a conquering nation imposes its language on subject peoples, so the Emperor of the heavenly city and the princes of His court decree that all who ascend there shall speak the language of the realm.

> Dunque essa è particolarmente adatta: così come una nazione vincitrice non solo impone il proprio giogo ai popoli sottomessi, ma assai spesso anche la propria lingua, così l'Imperatore della città superna e i principi della Sua corte, stabiliscono che tutti coloro che ascenderanno a quelle altezze parlino la lingua che di là proviene e della quale si serviva il principe di quel regno, quando viveva tra gli uomini.

<cite>p. 82</cite>

## Zorzi and Avraham De Balmes: A Surprising Convergence

Campanini draws attention to a significant intertextual parallel: Zorzi's arguments closely echo the first chapter of the Hebrew-Latin grammar *Miqneh Avram* (*Peculium Abrae*) by Avraham De Balmes, a Venetian Jewish scholar who was otherwise a committed Aristotelian and Averroist — philosophically antipodal to Zorzi. Despite this fundamental disagreement, both authors make virtually identical claims about the originality of Hebrew and both invoke the *Cratylus* to argue that authentic etymology is only possible in Hebrew (pp. 96–97, n. 351). Both also allude to the celestial alphabet (n. 353) written on the throne of God and model of the Mosaic Torah, and Campanini notes De Balmes's reference to an *angelico* alphabet and the "alphabet of the river crossing" (*del transito del fiume*) found in a very ancient book (*libro vetustissimo*, n. 354). The passage also gives voice to the conviction — expressed by Daniel Bomberg in his preface to the *Peculium Abrae* — that even the Gospels were originally composed in Hebrew (p. 97, n. 355–356).

## Broader Context: Humanist Genealogies of Wisdom

Campanini situates Zorzi's Hebraism within the larger Renaissance quest for linguistic origins. The Humanist recovery of Latin gave way to the privileging of Greek (through Bessarione and Ficino); the next stage brought Egypt into prominence, through Horapollo's *Hieroglyphica* and then through Giordano Bruno's mythological-philosophical Egypt, culminating in the *Egyptomania* of Athanasius Kircher (p. 98). For Zorzi, however, Hebrew occupies a structurally different position: it is not merely the oldest recoverable stage of human language but simultaneously the origin and the eschatological horizon of all knowledge. The nostalgia for a recomposed, undivided *sapientia* — free of contradictions and fractures — finds its concrete expression in the Hebrew Bible, whose words, interpretation, and even graphic form preserve, unchanged, the score (*spartito*) on which the harmony of the world first sounded, sounds still (for those with ears to hear), and will sound definitively in the *città futura*, forever.

## Technical Terms

- **Prima lingua / lingua adamitica**: the original Adamic language, Hebrew
- **Protoplastes**: the first creature (Adam), who named all things
- **Consequentia rerum**: the congruence between words and things, preserved intact in Hebrew
- **Secretiores theologi Hebraeorum**: the kabbalists; Zorzi's standard term for esoteric Jewish theologians
- **'Abar** (עבר): Hebrew root meaning "to pass over," etymological basis of the name *Hebrew*
- **Miqneh Avram / Peculium Abrae**: the Hebrew-Latin grammar of Avraham De Balmes, printed by Daniel Bomberg in Venice
- **Spartito**: Campanini's metaphor — Hebrew as the "musical score" of cosmic harmony
